getting-fluent-in-tvl-first-contact.title: First Contact
getting-fluent-in-tvl-first-contact.description: Your first TVL specification: understanding the basic structure and syntax.
content: Welcome to your first hands-on encounter with TVL! This section introduces you to a minimal but complete TVL specification that demonstrates all the core concepts you'll need to get started.

TVL specifications are written in YAML format and consist of several key sections: spec metadata, configuration space, objectives, optimization settings, constraints, evaluation workloads, and promotion policies.

The example below shows a complete 'Hello TVL' specification that defines a simple AI pipeline configuration with two model choices, a temperature parameter, and token limits. This specification is production-ready and can be used with the Traigent optimization platform.

Each section serves a specific purpose: the spec section identifies your configuration, metadata provides ownership and documentation, configuration_space defines the tunable parameters, objectives specify what you want to optimize, and constraints ensure safety boundaries.

codeExample.code:
spec:
  id: hello-tvl
  version: 0.0.1
metadata:
  owner: "students@traigent.ai"
  description: "Minimal TVL spec used in Chapter 2 to introduce the core sections."
  reviewers: ["mentor@traigent.ai"]
units:
  time: [milliseconds, seconds]
  ratio: [dimensionless]
configuration_space:
  model:
    type: categorical
    values: ["gpt-4o-mini", "claude-3-haiku"]
  temperature:
    type: continuous
    range: [0.0, 0.8]
    unit: ratio
  max_tokens:
    type: discrete
    values: [256, 384, 512]
    unit: tokens
objectives:
  - name: faithfulness
    direction: maximize
  - name: latency
    direction: minimize
optimization:
  strategy: pareto_optimal
  budget:
    max_trials:
      value: 30
      unit: count
    timeout_seconds: 900
constraints:
  - id: mild-temperature
    type: expression
    rule: 'params.temperature <= 0.8'
    error_message: "Stick to milder sampling in the quickstart."
evaluation:
  workloads:
    - name: tutorial
      dataset: dvl://demo/tutorial
promotion:
  gate: manual_review
  reviewers: ["mentor@traigent.ai"]

getting-fluent-in-tvl-validation-warm-up.title: Validation Warm-Up
getting-fluent-in-tvl-validation-warm-up.description: Introduction to TVL validation tools and workflows.
content: Once you've written a TVL specification, the next step is validating it to ensure correctness. This section introduces you to programmatic validation using Python, demonstrating how to load, inspect, and verify TVL specifications.

The validation script below shows a practical approach to TVL spec validation. It loads the YAML file, checks for required sections, and prints out the configuration space in a human-readable format. This is the foundation for more sophisticated validation workflows.

Key validation steps include: checking that all required sections are present (spec, metadata, configuration_space, objectives, optimization), verifying that parameter types are correctly specified, ensuring constraints are syntactically valid, and confirming that objectives have proper directions.

This validation approach can be integrated into your CI/CD pipeline, allowing you to catch specification errors early in the development process. As you progress, you'll learn about more advanced validation techniques including constraint satisfaction checking and schema validation.

getting-fluent-in-tvl-a-minimal-working-.title: A Minimal Working Spec
getting-fluent-in-tvl-a-minimal-working-.description: Building a complete, minimal TVL specification that demonstrates core concepts.
content: A minimal working TVL specification is the foundation for understanding how all the pieces fit together. This section walks you through building a complete specification from scratch, explaining each required section and why it matters.

The key to a minimal spec is including only what's necessary while maintaining completeness. You need: spec identification, metadata for ownership, at least one configuration parameter, one objective to optimize, and basic optimization settings. Everything else is optional but recommended for production use.

Starting with the spec section, you declare a unique identifier and version number. This allows the Traigent platform to track your configuration over time and manage multiple versions. The metadata section provides context: who owns this spec, what it does, and who should review changes.

The configuration_space is where you define your tunable parameters. Even a single parameter like 'model' or 'temperature' is enough to get started. Each parameter needs a type (categorical, continuous, or discrete) and appropriate constraints. The objectives section tells the optimizer what to maximize or minimize, such as accuracy, latency, or cost.

codeExample.code:
spec:
  id: minimal-example
  version: 1.0.0
metadata:
  owner: "your-team@company.com"
  description: "Minimal TVL specification"
configuration_space:
  model:
    type: categorical
    values: ["gpt-4o-mini", "claude-3-haiku"]
objectives:
  - name: accuracy
    direction: maximize
optimization:
  strategy: pareto_optimal
  budget:
    max_trials:
      value: 10
      unit: count

getting-fluent-in-tvl-line-by-line-walkt.title: Line-by-Line Walkthrough
getting-fluent-in-tvl-line-by-line-walkt.description: Detailed explanation of each component in a TVL specification.
content: Understanding every line of a TVL specification is crucial for writing effective configurations. This section provides a detailed walkthrough of the hello-tvl example, explaining the purpose and syntax of each section.

The spec section begins with 'id' and 'version'. The id must be unique across your organization and follow kebab-case naming (lowercase with hyphens). The version follows semantic versioning (major.minor.patch), allowing you to track breaking changes, new features, and bug fixes separately.

In the metadata section, the owner field specifies who is responsible for this configuration. This is typically an email or team identifier. The description provides human-readable context, and the reviewers array lists who must approve changes before they're promoted to production.

The units section is optional but highly recommended. It defines custom unit systems for your parameters, such as time (milliseconds, seconds) or ratio (dimensionless). This ensures type safety and prevents unit confusion errors.

The configuration_space section is the heart of your spec. Each parameter has a name, type, and constraints. Categorical parameters list discrete choices, continuous parameters define numeric ranges, and discrete parameters specify allowed numeric values. Units can be attached to numeric parameters for dimensional analysis.

Objectives define what you want to optimize. Each objective has a name matching your evaluation metrics and a direction (maximize or minimize). The optimization section specifies the strategy (pareto_optimal for multi-objective optimization) and budget constraints like max_trials and timeout.

Constraints are safety nets. They use expression syntax to enforce rules like 'temperature must be below 0.8' or 'cost must stay under budget'. Each constraint has an id, type, rule expression, and error_message to guide users when violations occur.

The evaluation section defines workloads—datasets and test scenarios used to measure your objectives. The promotion section controls how configurations move from development to production, specifying gates (manual_review, automated_test) and required reviewers.

getting-fluent-in-tvl-typing-your-variab.title: Typing Your Variables
getting-fluent-in-tvl-typing-your-variab.description: Understanding TVL's type system for tuning variables, including categorical and continuous ranges.
content: TVL's type system is designed specifically for optimization and tuning, distinct from traditional programming language types. Understanding these types is essential for defining effective configuration spaces that the optimizer can explore efficiently.

Categorical types represent discrete choices with no inherent ordering. Examples include model names ('gpt-4o-mini', 'claude-3-haiku'), prompt strategies ('zero-shot', 'few-shot', 'chain-of-thought'), or output formats ('json', 'xml', 'yaml'). The optimizer treats these as independent options to try.

Continuous types define numeric ranges with infinite precision within bounds. Temperature (0.0 to 1.0), learning rates (0.0001 to 0.1), and probability thresholds are continuous. The optimizer can sample any value within the range, making gradient-based optimization possible.

Discrete types specify allowed numeric values from a finite set. Token limits (256, 512, 1024, 2048) or batch sizes (16, 32, 64) are discrete. Unlike continuous types, the optimizer can only choose from the explicit values you provide, which is useful when only certain values are valid.

Type selection impacts optimization efficiency. Categorical types require exhaustive or sampling-based search. Continuous types enable gradient descent and Bayesian optimization. Discrete types fall in between, allowing ordered search strategies. Choose the type that best represents your parameter's true nature.

Units add semantic meaning to numeric types. Attaching 'milliseconds' to a latency parameter or 'tokens' to a length parameter enables dimensional analysis. TVL can catch errors like adding a time value to a token count, preventing subtle bugs in constraint expressions.

codeExample.code:
configuration_space:
  # Categorical: discrete choices
  model:
    type: categorical
    values: ["gpt-4o-mini", "claude-3-haiku", "gemini-1.5-flash"]
  
  # Continuous: numeric range
  temperature:
    type: continuous
    range: [0.0, 1.0]
    unit: ratio
  
  # Discrete: specific numeric values
  max_tokens:
    type: discrete
    values: [256, 512, 1024, 2048]
    unit: tokens
  
  # Continuous with unit
  timeout:
    type: continuous
    range: [100, 5000]
    unit: milliseconds

getting-fluent-in-tvl-common-pitfalls.title: Common Pitfalls
getting-fluent-in-tvl-common-pitfalls.description: Avoiding common mistakes when writing TVL specifications.
content: Even experienced developers make common mistakes when writing TVL specifications. This section highlights the most frequent pitfalls and how to avoid them, saving you debugging time and preventing production issues.

Pitfall 1: Overly broad configuration spaces. Defining temperature from 0.0 to 2.0 or including dozens of model choices creates an enormous search space that's impossible to explore efficiently. Start narrow and expand only when needed. Use constraints to enforce reasonable bounds.

Pitfall 2: Conflicting objectives without clear priorities. Maximizing accuracy while minimizing latency and cost creates a three-way tradeoff. The optimizer will find Pareto-optimal solutions, but you need to understand the tradeoffs. Consider using weighted objectives or constraints to express hard requirements.

Pitfall 3: Missing or incorrect units. Forgetting to specify units on numeric parameters leads to dimensional analysis errors in constraints. Worse, using the wrong unit (seconds vs milliseconds) causes silent bugs. Always declare units explicitly in the units section and attach them to parameters.

Pitfall 4: Insufficient optimization budget. Setting max_trials to 5 for a configuration space with 100+ combinations won't find good solutions. A rule of thumb: budget at least 10× the number of parameters for simple spaces, more for complex ones. Use timeout_seconds as a safety net.

Pitfall 5: Weak or missing constraints. Without constraints, the optimizer might find 'optimal' solutions that violate business rules or safety requirements. Always encode hard requirements as constraints, not just as objectives to minimize.

Pitfall 6: Incorrect evaluation datasets. Using a tiny or non-representative dataset leads to overfitting and poor production performance. Ensure your evaluation workloads match real-world usage patterns and include edge cases.

Pitfall 7: Skipping promotion gates. Pushing configurations directly to production without review or testing is dangerous. Always use promotion gates (manual_review or automated_test) to catch issues before they impact users.

getting-fluent-in-tvl-objectives-and-bud.title: Objectives and Budgets
getting-fluent-in-tvl-objectives-and-bud.description: Defining optimization objectives and resource constraints.
content: Objectives tell the optimizer what to optimize, while budgets control how much computational resources to spend. Getting both right is essential for effective automated tuning.

Objectives must be measurable, meaningful, and aligned with business goals. Common objectives include accuracy metrics (precision, recall, F1), latency (p50, p95, p99), cost (dollars per request, token usage), and quality scores (faithfulness, relevance, coherence). Each objective needs a clear direction: maximize or minimize.

Single-objective optimization is straightforward: the optimizer finds the configuration that maximizes (or minimizes) your metric. Multi-objective optimization is more complex: you're trading off competing goals. The optimizer returns a Pareto frontier—configurations where improving one objective requires sacrificing another.

Budget constraints prevent runaway optimization costs. The max_trials parameter limits how many configurations to evaluate. Set this based on your configuration space size and available compute. The timeout_seconds parameter provides a hard deadline, useful for time-sensitive tuning.

Advanced budget controls include early stopping (halt if no improvement after N trials), resource limits (max GPU hours, max API calls), and adaptive budgets (spend more on promising regions). These help balance exploration (trying diverse configurations) with exploitation (refining good ones).

Choosing the right optimization strategy depends on your objectives. Use 'pareto_optimal' for multi-objective problems, 'bayesian' for expensive evaluations with few trials, 'grid_search' for exhaustive exploration of small spaces, or 'random_search' as a baseline.

codeExample.code:
objectives:
  - name: faithfulness
    direction: maximize
  - name: latency
    direction: minimize
  - name: cost_per_request
    direction: minimize

optimization:
  strategy: pareto_optimal
  budget:
    max_trials:
      value: 50
      unit: count
    timeout_seconds: 3600
  early_stopping:
    patience: 10
    min_improvement: 0.01

getting-fluent-in-tvl-promotion-as-a-fir.title: Promotion as a First-Class Citizen
getting-fluent-in-tvl-promotion-as-a-fir.description: Understanding how configuration promotion works in TVL.
content: TVL treats configuration promotion as a first-class concept, not an afterthought. This reflects the reality that moving configurations from development to production is a critical, high-risk operation that requires careful governance.

The promotion section defines gates—checkpoints that configurations must pass before advancing to the next environment. Manual review gates require human approval from designated reviewers. Automated test gates run validation suites to catch regressions. Canary gates deploy to a small percentage of traffic before full rollout.

Reviewers are specified by email or team identifier. When a configuration is ready for promotion, the system notifies reviewers and provides a diff showing what changed. Reviewers can approve, reject, or request modifications. This creates an audit trail for compliance and debugging.

Promotion policies can be environment-specific. Development might allow automatic promotion, staging might require one reviewer, and production might require two reviewers plus automated tests. This balances velocity in early stages with safety in production.

Integration with CI/CD pipelines is seamless. TVL specifications can be version-controlled alongside code, and promotion gates can trigger deployment workflows. This enables GitOps-style configuration management where every change is tracked, reviewed, and reversible.

Rollback capabilities are built-in. If a promoted configuration causes issues, you can instantly revert to the previous version. The promotion history provides a complete audit trail, making it easy to identify when problems were introduced and by whom.

codeExample.code:
promotion:
  gate: manual_review
  reviewers:
    - "tech-lead@company.com"
    - "ml-team@company.com"
  environments:
    - name: development
      gate: automatic
    - name: staging
      gate: manual_review
      reviewers: ["tech-lead@company.com"]
    - name: production
      gate: manual_review
      reviewers:
        - "tech-lead@company.com"
        - "product-manager@company.com"
      require_tests: true

constraints-units-safety-nets-why-units-.title: Why Units Matter
constraints-units-safety-nets-why-units-.description: Understanding the importance of units in AI pipeline configurations.
content: In traditional software, mixing units causes catastrophic failures—think NASA's Mars Climate Orbiter, lost because one team used metric and another used imperial units. AI pipelines face the same risk, but the consequences are subtler and harder to detect.

Consider a timeout parameter: is it in milliseconds or seconds? A latency budget: milliseconds or microseconds? A cost constraint: dollars per request or cents per thousand tokens? Without explicit units, these ambiguities lead to silent bugs that only surface in production when your optimizer finds 'optimal' configurations that violate implicit assumptions.

TVL's unit system provides dimensional analysis for AI parameters. You declare unit families (time, currency, ratio) and attach units to parameters. The constraint engine then catches unit mismatches at validation time, preventing errors like comparing a millisecond timeout to a second threshold or adding a cost in USD to a latency in milliseconds.

Units also improve readability and maintainability. When you see 'cache_ttl_hours: 2.0', the unit is explicit in both the parameter name and the type system. When constraints reference this parameter, the unit checking ensures all comparisons are dimensionally consistent.

Beyond error prevention, units enable semantic reasoning. The optimizer can convert between compatible units (milliseconds to seconds), normalize values for comparison, and enforce domain-specific rules like 'all latencies must be under 500ms' without ambiguity.

constraints-units-safety-nets-cel-in-pla.title: CEL in Plain English
constraints-units-safety-nets-cel-in-pla.description: Introduction to Common Expression Language (CEL) for encoding business and safety rules.
content: Common Expression Language (CEL) is TVL's constraint grammar. It's a simple, safe expression language designed for evaluating user-defined logic without the security risks of full programming languages. CEL expressions are deterministic, side-effect-free, and guaranteed to terminate.

CEL syntax resembles Python or JavaScript but is more restricted. You can use arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /), comparison operators (<, <=, >, >=, ==, !=), logical operators (&&, ||, !), and access object fields with dot notation (params.temperature). Functions like size(), matches(), and contains() provide common operations.

In TVL, CEL expressions reference configuration parameters via the 'params' object. For example, 'params.temperature < 0.8' checks that temperature stays below 0.8. 'params.temperature * params.max_tokens <= 600' enforces a product constraint. These expressions are evaluated during optimization to filter out invalid configurations.

CEL's safety guarantees are crucial for production systems. Expressions can't perform I/O, modify state, or run indefinitely. This makes CEL ideal for user-provided constraints that need to run in a secure, sandboxed environment. The Traigent platform evaluates CEL expressions millions of times during optimization without security concerns.

TVL supports three constraint types using CEL: expression constraints (must be true), conditional constraints (if-then rules), and forbidden constraints (must be false). This covers most business logic: hard requirements, conditional rules based on other parameters, and explicit prohibitions on dangerous configurations.

constraints-units-safety-nets-worked-exa.title: Worked Example
content: Let's walk through a real-world example that demonstrates units, constraints, and safety nets working together. This specification manages a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline with four tunable parameters: temperature, max_tokens, cache_ttl_hours, and rerank_weight.

The units section declares four unit families: time (milliseconds, seconds), currency (USD), ratio (dimensionless), and percentage (percent). Each parameter in the configuration space gets a unit annotation: temperature and rerank_weight use 'ratio', max_tokens uses 'tokens', and cache_ttl_hours uses 'hours'.

The first constraint, 'cache-ttl-unit-check', enforces a minimum cache duration: 'params.cache_ttl_hours >= 1.0'. This prevents the optimizer from choosing sub-hour cache durations that would cause excessive cache churn and database load.

The second constraint, 'token-temperature-budget', enforces a product rule: 'params.temperature * params.max_tokens <= 600'. This empirically-derived rule prevents hallucinations by limiting the combination of high temperature (which increases randomness) and long outputs (which compound errors).

The third constraint, 'rerank-mandate', is conditional: when max_tokens >= 512, then rerank_weight >= 0.4. This encodes domain knowledge: longer outputs benefit more from reranking, so we require stronger reranking when generating long responses.

The fourth constraint, 'rerank-cap', is a forbidden rule: 'params.rerank_weight > 0.8' must be false. This prevents over-reliance on reranking, which our team found causes instability in production. By encoding this as a constraint rather than just limiting the range, we document the business reason and make it testable.

codeExample.code:
spec:
  id: constraints-lab
  version: 0.1.0
metadata:
  owner: "qa@traigent.ai"
  description: "Explores CEL constraints and unit typing."
units:
  time: [milliseconds, seconds]
  currency: [USD]
  ratio: [dimensionless]
  percentage: [percent]
configuration_space:
  temperature:
    type: continuous
    range: [0.0, 1.0]
    unit: ratio
  max_tokens:
    type: integer
    range: [128, 1024]
    unit: tokens
  cache_ttl_hours:
    type: continuous
    range: [0.5, 12.0]
    unit: hours
  rerank_weight:
    type: continuous
    range: [0.0, 1.0]
    unit: ratio
objectives:
  - name: stability
    direction: maximize
  - name: cost
    direction: minimize
constraints:
  - id: cache-ttl-unit-check
    type: expression
    rule: 'params.cache_ttl_hours >= 1.0'
    error_message: "Cache needs at least 1 hour TTL."
  - id: token-temperature-budget
    type: expression
    rule: 'params.temperature * params.max_tokens <= 600'
    error_message: "Token-temperature product keeps hallucinations manageable."
  - id: rerank-mandate
    type: conditional
    when: 'params.max_tokens >= 512'
    then: 'params.rerank_weight >= 0.4'
    error_message: "Long answers must lean on reranking."
  - id: rerank-cap
    type: forbidden
    rule: 'params.rerank_weight > 0.8'
    error_message: "Rerank weight above 0.8 is unstable."

constraints-units-safety-nets-constraint.title: Constraint Playbook
constraints-units-safety-nets-constraint.description: Best practices and patterns for writing effective constraints.
content: Writing effective constraints is both an art and a science. Good constraints encode domain knowledge, prevent production incidents, and guide the optimizer toward safe configurations. Poor constraints are either too restrictive (eliminating good solutions) or too permissive (allowing dangerous ones).

Pattern 1: Range guards. Use simple bounds to enforce hard limits: 'params.temperature <= 0.9', 'params.latency_ms <= 500', 'params.cost_per_request <= 0.05'. These are your first line of defense against obviously bad configurations.

Pattern 2: Product constraints. Encode interactions between parameters: 'params.temperature * params.max_tokens <= 600', 'params.batch_size * params.num_workers <= 1000'. These capture empirical relationships discovered through experimentation.

Pattern 3: Conditional rules. Express context-dependent requirements: 'if params.use_cache then params.cache_ttl >= 60', 'if params.model == "gpt-4" then params.max_tokens <= 8192'. These handle parameter interdependencies.

Pattern 4: Budget constraints. Enforce resource limits: 'params.cost_per_request * params.expected_qps <= daily_budget / 86400', 'params.latency_p99 <= sla_threshold'. These ensure your configuration stays within operational bounds.

Pattern 5: Safety prohibitions. Explicitly forbid known-bad configurations: 'params.temperature > 1.5', 'params.top_p == 0.0', 'params.frequency_penalty < -2.0'. Document why these are forbidden in the error_message.

Best practice: Start with loose constraints and tighten based on production data. Monitor constraint violation rates during optimization. If a constraint never triggers, it might be redundant. If it triggers constantly, it might be too strict or incorrectly specified.

Anti-pattern: Don't use constraints to encode preferences. Use objectives for that. Constraints are for hard requirements that must never be violated. If you want to minimize cost, make it an objective, not a constraint like 'params.cost <= some_value'.

constraints-units-safety-nets-making-con.title: Making Constraints Testable
constraints-units-safety-nets-making-con.description: Strategies for testing and validating your constraints.
content: Constraints are code, and like all code, they need tests. Untested constraints are a liability: they might have logic errors, edge cases, or unit mismatches that only surface during optimization or in production.

The simplest testing approach is unit testing with concrete parameter values. For each constraint, write tests that verify it passes with valid configurations and fails with invalid ones. This catches basic logic errors and ensures your CEL expressions are syntactically correct.

Property-based testing takes this further by generating random parameter combinations and checking that constraints behave consistently. This discovers edge cases you might not think of: boundary values, extreme combinations, or interactions between constraints.

Integration testing validates constraints against real optimization runs. Run a small optimization trial and verify that all returned configurations satisfy your constraints. If the optimizer returns constraint-violating configurations, you have a bug in either your constraints or the constraint evaluation logic.

Regression testing ensures constraints don't break when you modify them. Maintain a test suite with known-good and known-bad configurations. When you update a constraint, run the suite to verify you didn't accidentally relax or over-tighten the rule.

The example test file demonstrates pytest-based constraint testing. It loads the TVL specification, extracts constraint expressions, and evaluates them with test parameter sets. This approach integrates naturally into CI/CD pipelines and provides fast feedback during development.

codeExample.code:
"""Example pytest module that exercises the Chapter 3 CEL constraints."""
from __future__ import annotations

from pathlib import Path
from types import SimpleNamespace
from typing import Any, Dict
import re

import yaml


SPEC_PATH = Path(__file__).with_name("ch3_constraints_units.tvl.yml")
SPEC = yaml.safe_load(SPEC_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))


def evaluate_constraint(expr: str, params: Dict[str, Any]) -> bool:
    """Tiny CEL-like evaluator for demonstration purposes."""
    python_expr = expr.replace("&&", " and ").replace("||", " or ")
    python_expr = re.sub(r"!(?!=)", " not ", python_expr)
    activation = {"params": SimpleNamespace(**params)}
    return bool(eval(python_expr, {}, activation))


def test_temperature_token_budget_passes() -> None:
    params = {
        "temperature": 0.5,
        "max_tokens": 512,
        "cache_ttl_hours": 2.0,
        "rerank_weight": 0.5,
    }
    expr = next(c["rule"] for c in SPEC["constraints"] if c["id"] == "token-temperature-budget")
    assert evaluate_constraint(expr, params)


def test_temperature_token_budget_fails() -> None:
    params = {
        "temperature": 0.95,
        "max_tokens": 768,
        "cache_ttl_hours": 3.0,
        "rerank_weight": 0.6,
    }
    expr = next(c["rule"] for c in SPEC["constraints"] if c["id"] == "token-temperature-budget")
    assert not evaluate_constraint(expr, params)

constraints-units-safety-nets-debugging-.title: Debugging Failures Fast
constraints-units-safety-nets-debugging-.description: Tools and techniques for troubleshooting constraint violations.
content: Constraint violations are inevitable during development and optimization. The key is diagnosing them quickly so you can fix the root cause rather than working around symptoms.

Start with the error message. TVL requires every constraint to have an error_message field that explains what went wrong and why the constraint exists. Good error messages include the constraint name, the violated rule, the actual parameter values, and guidance on how to fix it.

Use structured logging to capture constraint evaluations. Log every constraint check with the constraint ID, the parameters being evaluated, and the result (pass/fail). This creates an audit trail you can search when debugging production issues.

Visualization helps identify patterns. Plot parameter combinations that pass vs. fail constraints in 2D or 3D space. Clusters of failures often reveal underlying issues: maybe your constraint is too strict in one region, or maybe certain parameter combinations are fundamentally incompatible.

Binary search narrows down complex violations. If a configuration fails multiple constraints, disable half of them and re-test. Repeat until you isolate the problematic constraint. This is especially useful when constraints interact in non-obvious ways.

Constraint simulation lets you test 'what-if' scenarios. Given a configuration that violates a constraint, incrementally adjust parameters and observe when the constraint starts passing. This reveals the constraint's sensitivity and helps you understand its boundaries.

When all else fails, simplify. Remove all constraints except the one causing problems. Generate random configurations and check which ones pass. Analyze the passing configurations to understand what the constraint actually enforces vs. what you intended.

patterns-for-real-deployments-inheritanc.title: Inheritance Without Surprises
content: Configuration inheritance is a double-edged sword. Done well, it eliminates duplication and ensures consistency across environments. Done poorly, it creates hidden dependencies, unexpected overrides, and debugging nightmares. TVL's inheritance model is designed to be explicit, predictable, and auditable.

TVL uses the 'extends' keyword to create inheritance relationships. A child specification inherits all fields from its parent, then applies 'overrides' to customize specific values. This is similar to object-oriented inheritance but with stricter semantics: you can only override, not add new fields to inherited sections.

The key principle is 'no surprises': when you read a specification that extends another, you should be able to understand exactly what changed without hunting through multiple files. TVL enforces this by requiring explicit override declarations and prohibiting implicit field additions.

Inheritance chains are allowed but should be kept shallow. A common pattern is base → staging → production, where each level adds environment-specific constraints and narrows the configuration space. Deep inheritance hierarchies become hard to reason about and should be avoided.

TVL validates the entire inheritance chain at load time. If a child specification's overrides create invalid configurations (violating parent constraints, creating type mismatches, or producing empty configuration spaces), the specification is rejected with a clear error message pointing to the problematic override.

patterns-for-real-deployments-staging-vs.title: Staging vs. Production, Codified
patterns-for-real-deployments-staging-vs.description: Best practices for managing multiple deployment environments.
content: Most AI systems run in multiple environments: development for experimentation, staging for integration testing, and production for real users. Each environment has different constraints, risk tolerances, and optimization goals. TVL's environment system codifies these differences in a structured, version-controlled way.

The base specification defines the full configuration space and objectives. Environment-specific sections then narrow this space and add constraints. For example, staging might limit model choices to cheaper options and reduce optimization budget, while production adds latency thresholds and stricter promotion gates.

A key pattern is progressive constraint tightening: development has loose constraints to encourage exploration, staging adds safety rails to catch obvious problems, and production enforces strict SLAs and cost limits. This creates a funnel where only robust configurations make it to production.

Environment inheritance works naturally with TVL's extends mechanism. Production can extend staging, inheriting its constraints and then adding production-specific requirements. This ensures that any configuration valid in production is also valid in staging, making promotion safe and predictable.

The example specification shows a three-tier setup: base defines the full space, staging narrows model choices and temperature range, and production extends staging while adding latency thresholds and stricter promotion criteria. This pattern scales well to complex deployments.

patterns-for-real-deployments-constraint.title: Constraint-Aware Overlays
patterns-for-real-deployments-constraint.description: Applying configuration overlays that respect structural constraints.
content: Overlays are temporary or environment-specific modifications to a base specification. Unlike inheritance, which creates permanent relationships, overlays are applied at runtime to adapt configurations to specific contexts. TVL's overlay system ensures that overlays respect all constraints from the base specification.

When you apply an overlay, TVL first validates that the overlay's overrides are structurally compatible with the base: types must match, ranges must be subsets, categorical values must be subsets of base values. This prevents overlays from accidentally expanding the configuration space beyond what the base allows.

Constraint checking happens after overlay application. The merged specification must satisfy all constraints from both the base and the overlay. If an overlay creates constraint violations, the specification is rejected. This ensures overlays can't accidentally create invalid configurations.

A common use case is environment-specific overlays: the same base specification with different overlays for development, staging, and production. Each overlay narrows the space and adds environment-specific constraints, but all share the same core structure and validation logic.

Another pattern is feature flags via overlays: enable experimental features by applying an overlay that adds new parameters or relaxes constraints. When the experiment ends, remove the overlay to revert to the base behavior. This makes A/B testing and gradual rollouts straightforward.

codeExample.code:
spec:
  id: overlays-fleet
  version: 0.2.0
metadata:
  owner: "platform@traigent.ai"
  description: "Shows how extends/overrides manage environments."
units:
  time: [milliseconds, seconds]
  ratio: [dimensionless]
  currency: [USD]
configuration_space:
  model:
    type: categorical
    values: ["gpt-4o-mini", "claude-3-sonnet", "claude-3-haiku"]
  temperature:
    type: continuous
    range: [0.2, 0.9]
    unit: ratio
  max_tokens:
    type: integer
    range: [128, 1024]
    unit: tokens
  reranker:
    type: categorical
    values: ["e5-large", "voyage-2", "semantic-router"]
objectives:
  - name: helpfulness
    direction: maximize
  - name: latency
    direction: minimize
  - name: cost
    direction: minimize
optimization:
  strategy: pareto_optimal
  budget:
    max_trials:
      value: 80
      unit: count
    parallel_trials: 8
    timeout_seconds: 3600
environments:
  staging:
    overrides:
      configuration_space:
        model:
          values: ["gpt-4o-mini", "claude-3-haiku"]
        temperature:
          range: [0.2, 0.6]
      optimization:
        budget:
          max_trials:
            value: 30
            unit: count
  production:
    extends: staging
    overrides:
      configuration_space:
        max_tokens:
          range: [256, 768]
      objectives:
        - name: latency
          threshold:
            value: 750
            unit: milliseconds
      promotion:
        gate: strict_dominance

patterns-for-real-deployments-when-to-ex.title: When to Extend vs. Duplicate
patterns-for-real-deployments-when-to-ex.description: Deciding between extending existing specifications or creating new ones.
content: One of the most common questions when working with TVL is: should I extend an existing specification or create a new one from scratch? The answer depends on how much the new specification shares with the existing one and how tightly coupled you want them to be.

Extend when: (1) The new specification is a strict subset or refinement of the base. (2) Changes to the base should automatically propagate to the child. (3) The relationship is long-lived and represents a genuine 'is-a' relationship (e.g., production is a staging configuration with stricter constraints).

Duplicate when: (1) The specifications serve different purposes even if they look similar. (2) You want independent evolution—changes to one shouldn't affect the other. (3) The similarity is coincidental, not fundamental. (4) The configuration spaces might diverge significantly over time.

A middle ground is to extract shared components into a template or library specification that multiple independent specifications can reference. This captures commonality without creating tight coupling. TVL doesn't have first-class template support yet, but you can achieve this with tooling that generates specifications from shared fragments.

Anti-pattern: Don't use inheritance to share small fragments of configuration. If only 10% of the specification is shared, duplication is clearer than inheritance. Inheritance is for substantial shared structure, not minor convenience.

Best practice: Start with duplication, refactor to inheritance only when you have multiple specifications that genuinely need to stay synchronized. Premature abstraction through inheritance creates coupling that's hard to undo later.

patterns-for-real-deployments-optimizati.title: Optimization Playbooks
patterns-for-real-deployments-optimizati.description: Reusable patterns for common optimization scenarios.
content: Optimization playbooks are reusable patterns for common tuning scenarios. Instead of reinventing optimization strategies for each new pipeline, you can apply proven patterns that encode best practices and domain knowledge.

Playbook 1: Cost-constrained quality maximization. Fix a cost budget (e.g., $0.05 per request) and maximize quality metrics within that budget. Use a constraint to enforce the cost ceiling and a single quality objective. This is the most common pattern for production systems.

Playbook 2: Latency-constrained quality maximization. Similar to cost-constrained, but with a latency threshold (e.g., p95 < 500ms). Useful for user-facing applications where responsiveness matters more than cost.

Playbook 3: Pareto frontier exploration. Optimize multiple objectives simultaneously (quality, cost, latency) without hard constraints. The optimizer returns a set of Pareto-optimal configurations, and you choose based on current business priorities. This is ideal for exploratory analysis.

Playbook 4: Safety-first optimization. Start with very tight constraints (conservative model choices, low temperature, strict content filters) and gradually relax them while monitoring safety metrics. This is the right approach for high-stakes applications like medical or financial AI.

Playbook 5: Rapid iteration. Use small optimization budgets (10-20 trials) and fast feedback loops. Optimize frequently (daily or per-commit) rather than running expensive multi-day campaigns. This works well for systems with stable workloads and clear quality signals.

The key to playbooks is encoding them in TVL specifications that can be version-controlled, reviewed, and reused. Document why you chose specific constraints and objectives so future engineers understand the reasoning.

patterns-for-real-deployments-hotfix-ove.title: Hotfix Overlays
patterns-for-real-deployments-hotfix-ove.description: Emergency configuration patterns for production incidents.
content: Production incidents require fast, safe configuration changes. Hotfix overlays are temporary specifications that narrow the configuration space or add emergency constraints in response to outages, cost spikes, or quality regressions.

A hotfix overlay extends the production specification and applies strict overrides: limit model choices to known-stable options, reduce temperature to minimize variability, add cost caps to prevent runaway spending, or disable features that are causing problems.

The key property of hotfix overlays is that they're explicitly temporary. Include an 'expires_after' field with a date, and set up monitoring to alert when the overlay is approaching expiration. This prevents temporary fixes from becoming permanent technical debt.

Hotfix overlays should be minimal: change only what's necessary to mitigate the incident. Resist the temptation to 'improve' other things while you're making emergency changes. Each override increases the risk of unintended side effects.

After the incident is resolved, run a full optimization cycle without the hotfix overlay to find a permanent solution. The hotfix bought you time; use it to understand root causes and implement proper fixes rather than relying on the overlay indefinitely.

The example shows a hotfix overlay applied during a vendor outage. It restricts model choices to available providers, tightens temperature range to reduce variability, reduces optimization budget to save time, and adds a temporary cost cap. The 'expires_after' field ensures this doesn't become permanent.

codeExample.code:
extends: overlays-fleet
metadata:
  owner: "sre@traigent.ai"
  description: "Temporary cap to avoid premium model saturation during vendor outage."
  expires_after: "2025-01-15"
overrides:
  configuration_space:
    model:
      values: ["gpt-4o-mini", "claude-3-haiku"]
    temperature:
      range: [0.2, 0.5]
  optimization:
    budget:
      max_trials:
        value: 25
        unit: count
  constraints:
    - id: temporary-cost-cap
      type: expression
      rule: 'params.cost_usd <= 0.025'
      error_message: "Hold cost under temporary ceiling during outage."

patterns-for-real-deployments-provenance.title: Provenance and Promotion
patterns-for-real-deployments-provenance.description: Tracking configuration lineage and managing promotion workflows.
content: Provenance is the ability to trace where a configuration came from: which specification, which optimization run, which environment, and which approvals it received. This is critical for debugging production issues, auditing compliance, and understanding system behavior over time.

TVL specifications include metadata fields for owner, description, and version. These should be treated as first-class documentation, not afterthoughts. Every specification should clearly state who owns it, what it's for, and what version it is. This metadata travels with the configuration through the entire lifecycle.

Promotion is the process of moving a configuration from one environment to another (e.g., staging to production). TVL supports promotion gates: rules that must be satisfied before a configuration can be promoted. Common gates include manual review, automated testing, performance thresholds, and strict dominance (the new configuration must be better on all objectives).

A robust promotion workflow includes: (1) Automated validation (schema, constraints, unit tests). (2) Integration testing in staging. (3) Performance comparison against current production. (4) Manual review by designated approvers. (5) Gradual rollout with monitoring. (6) Automatic rollback on regression.

TVL's promotion section lets you specify gates, reviewers, and environment-specific promotion policies. This codifies your deployment process in the specification itself, making it auditable and version-controlled.

Best practice: Treat promotion as a first-class concern from day one. Don't wait until you have a production incident to think about how configurations move between environments. Build promotion workflows into your specifications and CI/CD pipelines from the start.

integration-patterns-unifying-the-stack.title: Unifying the Stack
integration-patterns-unifying-the-stack.description: How TVL integrates with the broader Traigent ecosystem.
content: TVL doesn't exist in isolation. It's the specification layer in a complete stack for governed AI adaptation. The Traigent ecosystem includes three core components: TVL for specification, Traigent (TVO) for optimization, and DVL for data validation. Together, they form a unified workflow from configuration to deployment.

TVL defines what can be tuned: the configuration space, constraints, objectives, and promotion policies. It's the contract between engineers and the optimization system. You write TVL specifications to declare your tuning problem, and the rest of the stack executes it.

Traigent (TVO - Traigent Variable Optimizer) is the optimization engine. It reads TVL specifications, runs experiments, and finds Pareto-optimal configurations. TVO handles the search strategy, trial scheduling, and objective evaluation. You don't write optimization code; you write TVL specs and let TVO do the work.

DVL (Data Validation Language) ensures measurement integrity. AI systems are only as good as their evaluation data. DVL lets you specify validation rules for datasets, detect drift, and enforce quality gates. This prevents garbage-in-garbage-out scenarios where optimization targets unreliable metrics.

The integration is seamless: TVL specifications reference DVL validation suites to ensure objectives are measured against clean data. TVO reads both TVL and DVL to orchestrate optimization campaigns. Deployment manifests tie everything together, creating an auditable trail from specification to production.

This unified stack addresses the full lifecycle of AI adaptation: define the problem (TVL), validate the data (DVL), optimize the solution (TVO), and deploy with confidence. Each component has a single responsibility, but they work together to solve the complete problem.

integration-patterns-nemo-optimizer-inte.title: NeMo Optimizer Integration
integration-patterns-nemo-optimizer-inte.description: Integrating TVL with NVIDIA's NeMo Agent Toolkit Optimizer.
content: NVIDIA's NeMo Agent Toolkit Optimizer is a powerful parameter optimization framework for AI agent workflows. It uses Optuna for numerical parameter tuning and genetic algorithms for prompt optimization. TVL specifications can serve as input to NeMo Optimizer, providing governed search spaces and constraints.

The integration between TVL and NeMo Optimizer is natural: both define search spaces for parameters, both support multi-objective optimization, and both aim for reproducible, governed optimization. TVL's configuration_space maps directly to NeMo's OptimizableField concept, where parameters are marked as tunable with associated SearchSpace definitions.

NeMo Optimizer uses Optuna for numerical parameters (temperature, max_tokens, top_p) and a custom genetic algorithm for prompt optimization. The GA evolves a population of prompt candidates over multiple generations using LLM-powered mutations. This aligns with TVL's support for both continuous/discrete parameters and categorical choices.

A typical integration workflow: (1) Write a TVL specification defining your configuration space, constraints, and objectives. (2) Export the TVL spec to NeMo's configuration format, mapping TVL's configuration_space to NeMo's OptimizableField declarations. (3) Run NeMo Optimizer with the exported configuration. (4) Validate NeMo's best_params.json output against TVL constraints to ensure compliance.

Key benefits of this integration: TVL provides governance and constraint enforcement, ensuring that NeMo's optimization stays within safe boundaries. NeMo provides advanced optimization algorithms (Optuna's TPE sampler, prompt GA) that complement TVL's specification framework. Together, they enable governed, reproducible optimization for complex AI agent workflows.

Example use case: Optimizing a RAG-based customer service agent. TVL specifies the search space (model choices, temperature range, retrieval parameters), constraints (latency < 500ms, cost < $0.10 per query), and objectives (helpfulness, factuality). NeMo Optimizer explores this space using Optuna, finds Pareto-optimal configurations, and returns best_params.json. The results are validated against TVL constraints before deployment.

Best practice: Use TVL as the source of truth for configuration governance, and NeMo Optimizer as the execution engine for optimization. Commit both TVL specifications and NeMo's best_params.json to version control. This creates an auditable trail from specification to optimized configuration, ensuring reproducibility and compliance.

integration-patterns-deterministic-artif.title: Deterministic Artifacts
integration-patterns-deterministic-artif.description: Ensuring reproducible configurations across the optimization lifecycle.
content: Reproducibility is critical for production AI systems. You need to know exactly which configuration is running, how it was generated, and how to recreate it if needed. TVL's deterministic artifact system ensures that every configuration can be traced back to its source and reproduced bit-for-bit.

The core artifact is the TVL lock file (tvl.lock). When you run 'tvl export --lock', TVL generates a lock file containing the fully resolved specification: all inheritance applied, all overlays merged, all defaults filled in. The lock file is self-contained and deterministic—given the same inputs, you always get the same lock file.

Lock files include cryptographic hashes (SHA-256) of the specification and all dependencies. This creates a tamper-evident chain: if any part of the specification changes, the hash changes, and you know something is different. This is crucial for auditing and compliance.

Provenance files (provenance.json) track the optimization history: which trials were run, which configurations were tested, which objectives were measured, and which configuration was ultimately selected. This creates an audit trail from specification to deployment.

Together, lock files and provenance files enable complete reproducibility. Given these artifacts, you can recreate the exact configuration, understand why it was chosen, and verify that it matches what's running in production. This is essential for debugging, compliance, and disaster recovery.

Best practice: Commit lock files and provenance files to version control alongside your TVL specifications. Treat them as first-class artifacts, not generated noise. They're the source of truth for what's actually deployed.

integration-patterns-handing-off-to-trai.title: Handing Off to Traigent
integration-patterns-handing-off-to-trai.description: Connecting TVL specifications to Traigent's optimization engine.
content: Once you've written a TVL specification, the next step is handing it off to Traigent (TVO) for optimization. This handoff is designed to be simple: you point TVO at your specification, and it handles the rest. But understanding what happens during the handoff helps you write better specifications.

TVO starts by validating your specification: schema compliance, constraint consistency, type correctness, and unit compatibility. If validation fails, TVO reports detailed errors pointing to the problematic sections. Fix the errors, re-run validation, and iterate until the specification is clean.

Next, TVO analyzes the configuration space to estimate search complexity. Large spaces (high-dimensional, many categorical values, complex constraints) require more trials and smarter search strategies. TVO uses this analysis to recommend optimization budgets and strategies.

TVO then generates a trial plan: which configurations to test, in what order, with what parallelism. The plan respects your optimization budget, constraints, and promotion gates. For Pareto optimization, TVO uses multi-objective search algorithms to explore the quality-cost-latency frontier.

During optimization, TVO runs trials, measures objectives, and updates the search strategy based on results. It respects constraints (invalid configurations are never tested), enforces budgets (stops when max_trials is reached), and handles failures gracefully (retries transient errors, skips persistent failures).

When optimization completes, TVO returns a set of candidate configurations (one for single-objective, multiple for Pareto). You review the candidates, choose one based on business priorities, and promote it through your deployment pipeline. TVO generates provenance files documenting the entire process.

The handoff is designed to be fire-and-forget: write a specification, run 'traigent optimize', and get back optimized configurations. But you can also run in interactive mode, inspect intermediate results, and adjust the search strategy mid-campaign if needed.

integration-patterns-lifecycle-of-a-prom.title: Lifecycle of a Promotion
integration-patterns-lifecycle-of-a-prom.description: Understanding the complete promotion workflow from optimization to production.
content: Promotion is the process of moving a configuration from optimization to production. It's not a single step; it's a multi-stage workflow with validation, testing, review, and deployment. TVL and Traigent provide the primitives to build robust promotion workflows.

Stage 1: Optimization. TVO runs trials in a development or staging environment, measures objectives, and produces candidate configurations. Candidates are stored with full provenance: which trials were run, which parameters were tested, which objectives were measured.

Stage 2: Validation. Candidates are validated against TVL constraints, DVL data quality checks, and integration tests. Invalid candidates are rejected immediately. Valid candidates proceed to performance testing.

Stage 3: Performance testing. Candidates are deployed to a staging environment and tested under realistic load. Key metrics (latency, throughput, error rate, cost) are measured and compared against current production. Candidates that regress on critical metrics are rejected.

Stage 4: Review and approval. Candidates that pass performance testing are reviewed by designated approvers (engineers, product managers, compliance officers). Reviewers check that the configuration aligns with business goals, compliance requirements, and operational constraints.

Stage 5: Gradual rollout. Approved candidates are deployed to production using gradual rollout strategies: canary deployments (1% of traffic), blue-green deployments (parallel environments), or feature flags (selective enablement). Monitoring watches for regressions.

Stage 6: Promotion or rollback. If the rollout succeeds (no regressions, positive user impact), the candidate is promoted to full production. If regressions are detected, the deployment is automatically rolled back to the previous configuration. Rollback is fast and safe because lock files ensure deterministic configurations.

TVL's promotion section lets you codify this workflow: specify promotion gates (strict_dominance, manual_review, performance_threshold), define rollback triggers (latency_regression, constraint_violation), and document approval requirements. This makes promotion repeatable, auditable, and safe.

integration-patterns-dvl-keeps-the-data-.title: DVL Keeps the Data Honest
integration-patterns-dvl-keeps-the-data-.description: Using Data Validation Language to ensure measurement integrity.
content: Optimization is only as good as the data you optimize against. If your evaluation dataset is biased, stale, or corrupted, optimization will find configurations that game the metrics without improving real-world performance. DVL (Data Validation Language) prevents this by enforcing data quality gates.

DVL is a declarative language for specifying validation rules on datasets. You write DVL suites that check for common data problems: missing values, outliers, schema drift, label imbalance, forbidden content (PII, profanity), and distribution shifts. DVL runs these checks before optimization and rejects bad data.

A typical DVL suite includes: (1) Schema validation (column types, required fields, value ranges). (2) Statistical checks (mean, variance, percentiles within expected ranges). (3) Drift detection (distribution hasn't shifted significantly from baseline). (4) Content validation (no PII, no toxic content, no adversarial examples).

DVL integrates with TVL through the objectives section. When you define an objective like 'helpfulness', you reference a DVL validation suite that ensures helpfulness scores are measured on clean, representative data. TVO won't run optimization until DVL validation passes.

This creates a quality gate: optimization only proceeds when data is trustworthy. If your evaluation dataset drifts (new user population, new topics, new failure modes), DVL detects it and blocks optimization until you update the dataset or adjust the validation rules.

DVL also supports continuous validation: run DVL checks on production traffic to detect data drift in real-time. If drift is detected, trigger re-optimization with updated data. This keeps your AI system adapted to changing conditions rather than optimized for stale benchmarks.

The integration between TVL and DVL is simple: TVL specifications reference DVL validation suites by path. TVO reads both, validates data before optimization, and includes DVL results in provenance files. This ensures that every optimized configuration is backed by validated data.

integration-patterns-a-manifest-that-glu.title: A Manifest That Glues It Together
content: The final piece of the integration puzzle is the deployment manifest. A manifest is a YAML file that ties together TVL specifications, Traigent optimization runs, DVL validation suites, and deployment targets. It's the single source of truth for what's deployed where and how it got there.

A typical manifest includes: (1) TVL spec path and SHA hash. (2) Lock file path for deterministic configuration. (3) Provenance file path for optimization history. (4) Traigent pipeline name and deployment target. (5) DVL dataset version and validation suite. (6) Integration checks and promotion gates.

Manifests are generated automatically by TVO after optimization completes. They capture the complete state of the deployment: which specification was used, which configuration was selected, which data was validated, and which checks passed. This creates an auditable record for compliance and debugging.

Manifests integrate seamlessly with CI/CD pipelines. You can write pipeline scripts that read manifests, run validation checks (tvl validate, dvl validate, traigent deploy --dry-run), and deploy configurations to production. The manifest ensures that all components are synchronized.

The example manifest shows a complete deployment: TVL spec for a RAG orientation pipeline, TVO optimization with strict dominance promotion gate, DVL validation suite for FAQ data with drift tests, and integration checks that validate the entire stack before deployment.

The example pipeline script shows how to use manifests in CI/CD: validate the TVL spec, export lock files, dry-run Traigent deployment, run DVL validation, and capture the manifest. This creates a repeatable, auditable deployment process that ties together all components of the stack.

Best practice: Commit manifests to version control alongside specifications and lock files. Treat them as the authoritative record of what's deployed. Use manifest diffs to understand what changed between deployments and why.

codeExample.code:
# Generated deployment manifest linking TVL, Triagent (TVO), and DVL.
manifest:
  spec_path: tvl/spec/rag-campus-orientation.tvl.yml
  spec_sha: 7c2f31be
  tvl_lock: tvl.lock
  provenance: provenance.json
triagent:
  pipeline: RAGOrientation
  optimizer: TVO
  deployment_target: production
  evaluation_job: dvc repro deploy-rag-orientation
  promotion_gate: strict_dominance
  rollback_on:
    - latency_regression
    - constraint_violation
dvl:
  dataset_version: faq/v2024.10
  drift_tests:
    - faq-topic-shift
    - forbidden-pii
  validation_suite: dvl suites/faq-orientation.json
integration_checks:
  - tvl validate tvl/spec/rag-campus-orientation.tvl.yml
  - tvl export --lock tvl/spec/rag-campus-orientation.tvl.yml
  - dvl validate suites/faq-orientation.json
  - triagent deploy --dry-run RAGOrientation

additionalExample.code:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

ROOT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
SPEC="${ROOT_DIR}/ch1_motivation_experiment.tvl.yml"
MANIFEST="${ROOT_DIR}/ch5_integration_manifest.yaml"

echo "[validate] Checking spec..."
tvl validate "${SPEC}"

echo "[export] Producing deterministic artifacts..."
tvl export --lock "${SPEC}"

echo "[triagent] Dry-running pipeline..."
traigent deploy --dry-run RAGOrientation

echo "[dvl] Running validation suites..."
dvl validate suites/faq-orientation.json

echo "[manifest] Capturing manifest..."
cat "${MANIFEST}"
